word_list.pronunciation: mˌeɪk ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ wˈɜːθ wˈʌnz wˌaɪl tˈaɪm

senses.definition: to make sure that something benefits one instead of just wasting one's time

word_list.pronunciation: dˈuː ˌɛsbˈiː ðɪ ɐ wˈɜːld ʌv ɡˈʊd

senses.definition: to make effort to make someone feel happier or healthier

examples.x: After struggling with depression for years, taking up a new hobby like painting might do Jane a world of good.

senses.definition: an acquaintance of one with great power or influence that can advance one's interests

examples.x: If the company faces legal trouble, having a friend at court with the judge could make a significant difference in the outcome.
The politician's friend in court helped him secure the nomination, and he was able to win the election with their support.

senses.definition: one's acquaintances who possess great influence and are willing to help one get out of trouble or achieve what one desires

examples.x: As a rising star in the industry, Jane is building relationships with executives to have friends in high places for future opportunities.
The entrepreneur's friends in high places helped her secure funding for her startup and launch it successfully.

senses.definition: something tangible and certain that one possesses, emphasizing the importance of appreciating and valuing it rather than seeking something else

examples.x: Although the investment opportunity seemed risky, Jack chose to invest as he believed that a bird in the hand was worth taking the chance.
While the promotion is not certain, John is hesitant to leave his current job as a bird in the hand is worth more than uncertain opportunities.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv sˈʌmθɪŋ ɡˌoʊɪŋ ˌɑːn ɔːɹ fɔːɹ ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to possess positive qualities or advantages that increase the likelihood of success or achievement in a particular area or situation

examples.x: The artist's unique style has something going on for her, making her artwork stand out from others.
The candidate's strong work experience had something going on for him when he applied for the job.

word_list.pronunciation: ɹˈaɪd ɐ ðə wˈeɪv ʌv ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to enjoy or benefit from a period of sudden success that something has brought

examples.x: By the end of the year, the team will have ridden the wave of productivity, completing several projects and meeting their targets.
The musician is riding a wave of creativity, with new song ideas constantly flowing and inspiring her.

senses.definition: to find oneself in a better condition or situation

examples.x: She decided she would be better off finding a new job.
They realized they were better off without the stressful commute.
They concluded that the company would be better off investing in new technology.
She knew she was better off after making healthier lifestyle choices.
He felt he would be better off saving money rather than spending it on unnecessary items.

senses.definition: to benefit one or improve one's situation in a variety of ways

examples.x: I told him that skipping breakfast every day wouldn't do him any good in the long run.
She realized that holding onto grudges wouldn't do her any good, so she decided to forgive and move on.
Constantly worrying about things that are out of your control doesn't do anyone any good; it's better to focus on what you can change.
Trying to finish the project in a rush won't do us any good; we need to take our time and do it right.

senses.definition: to be in an advantageous or favorable position, particularly financially

examples.x: After winning the lottery, the couple was sitting pretty and could afford to buy their dream home.
The company is sitting pretty in the market, with high demand for its products and a strong financial position.

senses.definition: to use something, such as one's skills, possessions, etc.

examples.x: John is putting his new camera to work by taking photos of his travels and sharing them on social media.
The company plans to put the new technology to work to improve its efficiency and reduce costs.
